Dearest Email Player,
Last summer I got this question from "Email Players'' subscriber Bjorgvin Benediktsson:

actual body of the emails, but I 	think my segues into

hint in that department?"

it's a great question, too. The only reason I've put off addressing it for so long is, I really had no
idea how to explain how I do it. It's always been a very organic, flowing process for me, and not
something I figured out by mechanically studying how others do it. So what I decided to do is go
through hundreds ~iterally) of my emails, especially the ones that did unusually well, and pull out
the best calls to action segues. What I would suggest you do is study them, and start applying
them (each one, one email at a time) to your emails. It WILL take some thinking and you WILL
likely have to play around with the words a bit. But this is one reason why I think swipe files are
BAD for email. It'd be like Rush Limbaugh reading off a script to close each radio show. Don't
make no sense. You cannot bore your readers and be predictable. But, if you adapt and modifY
these calls to action segues (switch out my info with yours), you'll have lots to choose from Qocked
in your subconscious) and be light years ahead of your inferior competition. OK, let's hit it ...

Dear Abby's
Multi-Million Dollar
E~nail Secret
One of the early "ah HA!" moments I had in copywriting was back in 2003-2004 (forget
when, exactly). I was reading an issue of "Men's Health" and came across an ad that looked just
like an article (one of those disguised ads with the word '~dvertisement" at the top so nobody
could complain about being fooled or whatever). Anyway, this ad was in there every month for a
long time. And when you read it, it sounded not like an ad or even an editorial piece or article. It
sounded like an advice column. It was obviously very effective, and it's something I use in
emails a lot these days (often in consumer markets). It's basically like your reader can "eavesdrop"
on some advice you're giving someone about how YOU solved the problem they are suffering
from. It doesn't feel like a sales pitch or look like a sales pitch, but it is indeed a sales pitch. And, if
you do it right, a very effective sales pitch that's hard for your readers to ignore and not want to
buy from. Below is an example of how you can do it. Simply modify and adapt with your product
details and market particularities, send to your list, and see what happens:
Subject line: Missing out on a better life due to weight gain
Hey,
I've seen it time and time and time again:
Friends, 	family and other people I 	know completely missing out on
a better life due to weight gain.
Take a friend I 	know, 	for example.
She • s 	a wreck!
And the reason why is her and her husband have been married for
almost 20 years. Yet, 	they only have pictures of them both
together from the first couple years of their marriage. And she
says they keep planning to go on a cruise one day, 	but she's
embarrassed to go due to her weight.
So every year they plan.
And every year they put it off until next year •••

Don't Necessarily
Poo-Poo ALL Surveys
A lot of direct marketing types poo-poo on sUiveys. They (rightfully) realize that what
people SAY they want and what they REALLY want are almost always two separate things. For
example, ask people if they prefer reading novels or magazines and they (to sound cool) will likely
say "novels of course!" But if you look at the last several things they read, chances are there will
be more magazines than novels on that list. Such is human nature. And such is the reason surveys
can be not only misleading but, as the GAP store found several years ago, even dangerous. But,
that doesn't mean you can't still get good (profitable!) intel from them. In fact, there is one kind of
survey that is perfect for using with emails. No survey software needed, and it's mostly reliable as
long as your customers are not all pathological liars. Anyway, the kind of survey I speaketh of is
asking them not what they like, but what they've recently BOUGHT. This is essentially what the
entire direct mail world is built on -- sending offers to lists of buyers for a particular product.
What we can do with email is similar. You simply send an email to your list asking them what the
last 3 products they bought were. This then gives you tremendous intel for how to sell them. You
can look those products up and see what kind of appeals they use in their ads. You can (and
should) also sign up on their email lists, too. See what they're doing right and wrong. Most
businesses -- even successful ones -- do a lot of things wrong, and are successful almost on
complete accident. For example, I did a bunch of sales letters in the self defense niche a couple
years ago. And the client found out through doing some very indepth market research that the
sales appeals and offers of his competitors (yes, even the big companies you see in magazines like
Black Belt, etc) were leaving mucho smackoloa on the table. So the ads I wrote were quite
different than the other ads in the industry. My point is, just because someone is successful doesn't
mean they aren't still screwing up. And the advantage you have as a subscriber to this newsletter,
is you can join most any email list and start seeing "gaps" in things they are doing, and could be
doing better. Then, all you have to do is take the stuff they're doing that's good and improve
upon it, giving you an instant advantage when selling to people who are on your lists and theirs.
Anyway, it's easy to do a survey email, too. In fact, in my humble (but accurate) opinion
it's much better than all these people using software like survey monkey or whatever anyway.
You simply send an email:
(1) Saying you're doing a survey because you want to better serve them and ...
(2) Asking them to send you the answers to the questions below by hitting reply
(much easier than them clicking over to a site, this is something most people

Subject line: 	Free "in store" credit
I want to do a quick survey.

Hit reply and send me the answer and I'll give you the credit.
You can use it on ANYthing I 	sell on my site.
And it's good forever.
(It doesn't expire.)

That's how simple it can be. And, of course, you can offer them anything (or nothing,
your call) you want for their cooperation. The power in this is two-fold: (I) you're only asking
them to do one task (answer ONE question) (2) the answer to that question will give you more
market intel than almost anything else you can do. After all, you'll know what products they've
spent MONEY on already. You'll see what sales appeals are working on them. And, you will see
other things too -- like prices, offers, website design, email appeals, and really the entire funnels of
the products they are buying.
Very simple.
Very powerful.
And, very profitable...

When To Ra~np Up
Your ''Asshole'' E~nails
A lot y'all have been asking me about ezine solo ads for getting traffic. In a future issue I
will get more indepth with some things I've learned about using ezine solo ads. But I'm still on
some learning curves myself, so am going to put it off a bit. However, there is something you can
use to profit from when generating large amounts of traffic (whether ezine ads or any other way).
I call them "asshole" emails.
Sounds terrible doesn't it?
Well, hold your horses,Jethro and I'll explain what I mean so there's some context. If you
are going to use solo ads (or any other kind of paid traffic) to generate opt-ins and not send them
directly to a sales pitch to turn them into buyers ASAP and not care about building an opt-in list
(a wise thing to do for many kinds of products and businesses), then you must realize you are
going to get a lot of people who are not REALLY prospects for your product. Especially if you
tell them they will get something free when they subscribe to your email list.
Mostly they are freebie seekers looking for free stuff.
Each list is different, of course.
But it's good to assume this unless you are mailing a really high quality list of buyers (and
there are ezines like that out there). And that being the case, it's in your best interest to drive the
freebie seekers (who will never buy, have no intention to buy, and will get hostile with you when
you ask them to buy) off your list as fast as possible. And, andgo, there is no better way I
know to do this than with cood old "asshole" eDla.ilS. For a few days or so after getting a
lot of traffic from any source, I will often do these kinds of emails. This is where I ramp up
getting controversial. Or will do some more blatant sales pitches. Or maybe even get outright
insulting towards the kind of people I don't want (i.e. mock freebie seekers right in the email, they
know who they are ...) The result is a mass exodus of subscribers who never had any intention of
buying from your righteous self anyway, and maybe some complaints, and a more "clean" list of
interested prospects for what you sell. Now realize, you're not doing this to be a dick. You're
doing this to help those who will just get mad being on your list when you ask them to buy each
day leave your list. In many ways, you are doing them a favor and service. You are also doing it to
keep your list stripped of people who will never buy and, if your list gets big enough, save you
money. (Since auto-responder services will start raising your rates when you get a certain amount
of leads in your system.)
-15-

So it's a win-win for everyone and a merry time will be had by all.
Anyway, this tip alone can save you a lot of grief and frustration. The reason why is you
may get a lot of traffic and leads, but if you don't take measures to turn off those who are NOT
good prospects, you will have a list with a lot of people who will just cost you money and
probably dump a load of frustration on you {people who will click the "spam" button out of spite,
etc, you want them OFF your list and out of your life ASAP).
